headline: Carlos can name wins, but his quality bar is unclear under time pressure—so feedback stays hard to translate into first actions.
summary_bullets: Carlos consistently ties performance to “concrete criteria,” saying broad feedback makes him “not sure what to change first.”
Under time pressure, he defaults to “polish” and loses the “core message,” which directly conflicts with his stated desire to improve concept rationale.
He asked for specificity (“clearer examples”) and a single practice (“one explicit priority rule”), but the conversation didn’t lock in the actual criteria or examples.
understanding_paragraph: Carlos is not struggling to do work—he’s struggling to know which dimension to prioritize when speed hits. He defines growth as faster alignment to concrete, junior-level excellence criteria, especially for concept rationale. The new thing is that his blocker is operational: broad feedback doesn’t produce a reliable “first action” for him.

axes.meaning: Weak signal: time pressure seems to degrade focus and confidence, worth watching for stress spillover.
No clear engagement signal: he’s receptive to feedback but his ownership is conditional on clarity.
Defining signal: the quality bar and “what to change first” are unclear, especially when reviews are broad.
Worth acting on: he’s blocked by lack of concrete criteria and wants a tighter loop on one skill at a time.

brutal_truth_employee: Carlos is stuck on translation, not effort: “When reviews are broad, I am not sure what to change first.” He also names the failure mode as execution triage: “I sometimes focus on polish and lose sight of the core message.”
brutal_truth_manager: Carlos gave you the exact lever—“align on concrete criteria”—but the conversation ended without you locking the criteria into examples and a first-action checklist. The moment to deepen was after he asked for “clearer examples of what excellent junior-level work looks like here,” because that’s where you should have turned feedback into an explicit rubric.

next_actions.action: Send Carlos 2-3 concrete examples of excellent junior-level work (with annotated callouts for concept rationale vs execution) and a short “what to change first” list.
Schedule a 30-minute calibration session where you and Carlos pick one time-pressure scenario and write the exact priority rule together, then test it on a past draft.
Start next 1:1 by asking Carlos to show the priority-rule he wrote and one before/after change tied to “core message” (not polish).

watch_for: Before next 1:1: if Carlos still says “not sure what to change first” after receiving examples, treat clarity as unresolved.
Within two weeks: if his drafts keep drifting to “polish” while “core message” slips, the priority rule isn’t specific enough.
